data.title: Mobileye: Stuck In Neutral
data.description:
Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY) is still struggling to transition from a leader in advanced driver assistance features for vehicles to fully autonomous driving. A prime issue is that the market has shifted to where passenger vehicles haven't moved forward with autonomous driving goals at the same speed as robotaxis, where Mobileye isn't as heavily involved. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, looking for an inflection point in the market.

Robotaxi Struggles

Mobileye is the global leader in technology and software for advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), primarily focused on the EyeQ SoC offering. The company generally operates up to the L2 level while the market is looking to shift to L4 robotaxis.

The company has a massive 8-year future expected automotive revenue pipeline of $24.5 billion, up 42% over the last couple of years. The pipeline amounts to ~$3 billion in annual revenue over the period compared to current annual revenues of nearly $2 billion.

Mobileye ships about 35 million EyeQ chips on an annual basis, but the issue is the transition from base ADAS services to AVs. The robotaxi world is moving full-speed ahead with Waymo, owned by Google (GOOG), WeRide (WRD), and potentially Tesla (TSLA), with Mobileye generally excluded from participating with the big players in this market.

The company has announced multiple deals with AV programs, but most of these deals aren't with market leading players. Moia, owned by Volkswagen Group (VWAGY), is working on a robotaxi in the Los Angeles market, where Waymo has fully operated since November 2024.

Mobileye likes to point out that Volkswagen Autonomous Mobility has plans to offer robotaxi services across 6 cities by the end of 2027 and target a fleet of 100,000 vehicles by 2033. Again, Mobileye has promising opportunities in the robotaxi space, but the technology and partners aren't seen as market leaders, and one really has to wonder if Moia has room for a large market position up to 7 years from now with the scaling of Waymo and potential size of Tesla with the Cybercab ready to start production of a $30K robotaxi this month.

Dead Money

The stock continues slumping to new all-time lows due to the precarious position of the business. The Robotaxi market is full-speed ahead, and Mobileye guided to 2026 revenues of flat to only up 5%.

The company has been stuck around the $2 billion sales levels for years now. The stock has a market cap of just $6 billion, or only 3x sales, due to this tepid outlook for AVs.

Berenberg just upgraded the stock with a $9.30 price target. Mobileye shareholders have mostly blown off the call due to the realization the stock started the year above $10, a higher level than this bullish call.

Mobileye is only forecast to produce an adjusted operating income of ~$200 million this year. The stock trades at about 30x this target, as the company is no longer highly profitable due to the need to invest in the business.

The company recently announced a deal to acquire Mentee Robotics to enter the humanoid robotics space. The deal has a cost of $900 million, comprising ~$612 million in cash and up to 26.2 million shares.

The humanoid robotics space is highly competitive, and Mobileye will need to invest heavily in a business competing with the likes of Tesla and a ton of Chinese operators. Musk has long announced plans to ramp up Optimus production to 1 million units, and Mobileye lacks the capital to compete in this space with better capitalized players.

Mobileye has a strong balance sheet with $1.84 billion in cash to end 2025. The Mentee Robotics deal will cut the cash balance to $1.23 billion, which is still a solid cash balance, but Mobileye has seen operating profits slip, and Mentee Robotics will boost operating expenses, probably materially, to have any real hope for success.

The stock isn't necessarily expensive now, trading at under 20x 2027 EPS targets of $0.38. The concern is Mobileye being dead money unless the robotaxi business ramps up, or passenger vehicle customers finally shift towards AV technologies.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Mobileye now operates in a couple of interesting categories, autonomous vehicle technology and humanoid robots, but the company is not a leader in either segment. Investors should continue watching the stock from the sidelines, looking for any competitive update suggesting a technology inflection is taking place to where the company will thrive in the ongoing technology shift. Otherwise, Mobileye will continue drifting lower.

data.title: Intrepid Potash: Iran-Driven Rally Looks Temporary (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Intrepid Potash (IPI), I reiterated their Buy rating, as the company continued to be in an exceptional financial position with virtually zero debt, highlighting their strategic importance in the face of macro uncertainty.

Following a boost from the Iran conflict that might turn out to be short-lived, I am downgrading IPI to a Hold now, as the stock is now trading around their well-deserved premium, and doesn’t offer an opportunity anymore, with macro pressure leading to higher near-term risks.

Excellent Foundation Intact

IPI reported another solid quarter, beating the market’s revenue and EPS expectations quite well, while the free cash flow reached about $25.54 million in 2025, lower than 2024 despite the drop in CAPEX, although a major reason behind this comes from the $11.37 million negative effect of accounts receivable, and they didn’t benefit from the deferred other income this time (related to the XTO Energy initial payment).

As a result of the replacement of a continuous miner at their East Underground Mine alongside higher operating hours per shift, the company's Trio production is expected to increase to about 285,000 to 300,000 tons this year (for a ~7% increase), while also deferring the decision regarding the AMAX cavern project until “at least 2027.”

Meanwhile, their Wendover (Utah) lithium project continues to advance, further boosting the company's portfolio of vital commodities, with their latest study indicating estimated measured and indicated maiden mineral resources of ~119,000 tons of lithium carbonate equivalent and 1.5 million tons of magnesium, potentially boosting this project into an excellent, vital multi-commodity operation (on top of the potash produced there), adding long-term potential as they’re expanding it.

As a note, they said this would come with a 25-year project life with a projected capacity of 5,000 tons per year of lithium carbonate, while also announcing a potential buyer for their South Ranch, indicating the potential of a definitive deal announcement in H1 2026 - on top of the potential XTO payments mentioned before.

Financially, based on IPI’s latest report, we continue to see an excellent balance sheet, with the current assets alone covering their total liabilities, while holding a strong ~$83.54 million in cash and cash equivalents, with equity now standing at $491.43 million compared to the $561.1 million market cap after the jump.

Intrepid expects CAPEX of $40 million to $50 million in 2026, of which about $40 million is their sustaining CAPEX, with their capital allocation priorities being sustaining higher production, strong balance sheet and liquidity, disciplined growth investments, and then opportunistic buybacks, while the XTO agreement [a subsidiary of Exxon Mobil (XOM)] can add significant extra capital that can also accelerate their capital returns to shareholders, as they say.

Don’t Get Too Excited About This Jump

Although this is likely clear for everyone, the recent jump in Intrepid’s price comes as a result of the war in Iran, as the Middle East is not only key for oil and gas, but it also plays a crucial role for the world’s fertilizer supply (especially nitrogen, urea, ammonia), being responsible for about a third of the globe’s fertilizer trade, so it’s no wonder why we see this type of jump.

Despite potash prices not moving much since the conflict escalated, IPI (which relies on potassium, magnesium, sulfur, and potentially lithium in the future) can gain from jumps in commodities such as nitrogen (including potential for their oilfield solutions business) since they are a domestic fertilizer producer that can benefit from a potential move from nitrogen into crops that require less of it and more of their products.

Still, I believe we’re talking about a near-term boost that can turn around to hurt them in the medium-term, as it might be short-lived regardless of the future of this conflict, as fundamentals were not that great in the near term even before.

Meanwhile, the current planting season is likely going to be affected significantly and result in even more inflationary pressure in the end, which the deputy executive director of the World Food Program, Carl Skau, also said recently:

In the worst case, this means lower yields and crop failures next season. In the best case, higher input costs will be included in food prices next year.

Although President Trump’s recent statements that indicate potentially a few weeks left to this conflict initially resulted in another boost for oil/gas/fertilizers to the detriment of precious metals, stocks, and treasuries, the long-term impact may not be that good for the demand.

This is something mentioned in more detail by Qatar’s Energy Minister and the head of ECB, who warned about the damage that’s already been done to the region’s energy infrastructure by Iran’s retaliation, which can result in higher war premiums for longer, potentially hurting the already weak demand even more.

At worst, this would result in much more than just a slowdown or an inflationary shock, which would be terrible for the demand of these commodities since the economy would be affected, while the prior fundamentals, especially when it comes to oil, were already weak. Especially after Trump’s threats to hit Iran “extremely hard” pushed commodity prices up once again, it’s important to keep in mind that every extra day at such high prices is ultimately bad for the demand, while it can also result in pressure on interest rates and affect the Fed’s decisions (alongside other central banks), and we see the market’s expectations of cuts in 2026 fading.

Meanwhile, even if we see a smooth recovery, fading war premiums would also potentially push these prices lower, meaning that unless another event that would somehow boost the demand or constrain the supply happens, we’re likely looking at lower prices in the foreseeable future, while potash’s fundamentals were also at risk in the near term due to risks of oversupply as projects such as BHP’s (BHP) Jansen could hit the market in about a year (after they delayed it).

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, since we’re talking about a commodity company, volatility is a given here, and the fact that the stock jumped following the Iran conflict shouldn’t be seen as a positive, as the risk is now significantly higher.

It’s true that the company remains a financial fortress, and this conflict may have helped the market realize its significant strategic importance given its portfolio’s geography and deserved “safe-haven” premium, but that doesn’t mean they are immune to the pressure that’s likely to come regardless of how this conflict advances.

Valuation

For IPI’s DCF to Equity valuation, without any change in net borrowings given their spotless balance sheet that doesn’t require that, taking into account the same commodity prices as in 2025, and their production guidance, but also the higher CAPEX expected in 2026, I’ll assume an FCF of $12.5 million in 2026.

This would be followed by a jump back to $20 million in 2027 thanks to CAPEX falling as Pond 8 wraps up and $40 million from 2028 as they can enter into the harvesting phase after these investments, growing at a 4.5% CAGR until year 10 given the potential from lithium, proceeds from Intrepid South, and the significant XTO optionality.

Regarding the latter, note that they're getting the $50 million access fee either when the project gets the approval for the first new/expanded drilling island OR in 7 years of the effective date of the amendment, meaning the beginning of 2031, plus up to $100 million depending on the project's activity.

This would then grow at a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 11% to take into account their potential volatility and risks associated with their projects/delays, as well as 13.12 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $476.65 million, for an intrinsic value of $36.33 per share, which is below the current $43.25 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

IPI is downgraded to a Hold, as the valuation now seems to account for a well-deserved premium, trading around what I’d call a fair value for a slightly more optimistic scenario than I’d like.

The company itself remains excellent, and I will continue to pay attention to them, but I wouldn’t call the current level attractive from a value point of view, while the Iran conflict that led to the boost also adds a lot more near-term risk.

data.title: Microsoft Has A Visibility Problem; I'd Rather Wait
data.description:
At first glance, based on the fact that Microsoft's (MSFT) fundamentals still look like a textbook high-quality name (and show no clear deterioration in recent quarters), a pullback may look like an obvious opportunity. But I’m not convinced the story is that straightforward.

Since I wrote my last article on MSFT in October last year, the stock has plummeted by about 29%.

But at that moment, prior to FQ1, I had outlined the following bear case:

Bearish case: Azure slowing to below 35%, below guidance, slower AI monetization, and excessive CapEx (15% increase QoQ) pressuring margins to ~42% or less. Copilot and E5 are stagnant, and Q2 guidance is conservative. EPS rising below ~11% YoY. The consequence would be forward earnings in the ~30x range, close to the floor, and a potential downside of 15%.

In short, the bear case did not materialize in either FQ1 or FQ2, but it came close in spirit. The growth of Azure falling below 35%, or the sharp drop in EPS, was not what actually materialized. In fact, the headline numbers remained quite strong.

But there was relative frustration vs. high expectations. The problem was more subtle than I anticipated. Instead, the market received a set of results that added more complexity than clarity—especially in terms of capital allocation. The contribution of AI within Azure and the timing of the accelerating CapEx cycle have also been detrimental to the MSFT trajectory in recent months.

So I would say that the question to be answered in FQ3 is not only whether AI will drive Microsoft, but when, and how clear that will show up in the numbers.

The Two-Act AI Story

Essentially, I would say that the performance of Microsoft stock today basically revolves around two key factors: (1) monetization of the AI infrastructure (including Azure and CapEx efficiency); and (2) monetization of AI at the application layer (including Copilot and pricing power Dynamics).

To make it clearer, the market has priced MSFT as the main "toll road" within AI. Therefore, what matters most is the speed of conversion of CapEx into revenue (the ROI of the data centers), alongside the supply vs. the capacity demand and incremental margin of this expansion, and not just the growth of Azure per se. I believe that, then, to a large extent, the faster Azure can turn billions in CapEx into revenue and margin, the better MSFT will go.

On the other hand, there is the "second act", which consists of transforming AI into ARPU (average revenue per user) real expansion within the Microsoft ecosystem (Office, Dynamics, etc.). In this specific context, the adoption of Copilot in terms of active usage (in addition to users) and the ability to charge a premium per subscription is evidence that AI is not just a cost and actually generates incremental revenues, but at the same time, in a sustainable way.

Azure Is Growing, But That’s Not the Point

Once we have put on the table what has arguably been driving MSFT shares, let's look to measure how that narrative around Azure and its key factors has developed recently.

I would put it first, in theory; what really matters would be to measure Azure growth ex-AI vs. AI contribution rather than Azure growth by itself. After all, if billions of CapEx are being dumped into AI, then investors want to see the AI percentage increase QoQ significantly.

But that's where the problem comes, since Microsoft hasn't detailed the Azure growth ex-AI, only that the total growth in the last quarter was 39% YoY, along with management comments of strong demand and capacity constraints, as usual.

Without this breakdown, the market is uncertain about how much of the growth in Azure is structural (AI) vs. cyclical/core cloud. Merely continuing to hit the key that "demand continues to exceed supply" suggests that AI is pulling hard, but how strong it gets is dubious, especially if capacity has been allocated outside of Azure with GPUs going to Copilot, R&D, etc., where it would be a sign that Azure growth is artificially capped.

The most revealing statement made by the management team in the last earnings call, being "If all GPUs were allocated to Azure, growth would be >40%," implies almost an "Azure ex-allocation constraint" proxy.

So here, as doubts arise whether Azure itself is a clean proxy for AI demand, part of the investment in infrastructure may be going to SaaS (Copilot), and this can be confusing for analysts to model the MSFT thesis in their spreadsheets and eventually revise numbers upwards.

Moving forward, the next step would be to measure CapEx vs. revenues to gauge the efficiency of the investment. In an ideal world, the healthy thing is that revenues grow proportionally to CapEx (or more). So naturally, when CapEx explodes, and revenue doesn't keep up, the red flag is raised.

In FQ2, Microsoft reported $37.5 billion in CapEx, two-thirds of that amount in short-term assets (GPUs/CPUs).

We can see that the market projections for the next three years at least show that revenues should grow in a very linear way (15-16% YoY), nothing explosive.

A superficial reading of CapEx jumping from ~$20-30 billion pre-2022 to over $80 billion in the last twelve months, indicates the opposite: explosive growth. So, at first glance, it seems to me that unless CapEx is expected to peak in 2026, the most obvious conclusion is ROI under intense pressure over the next three years at least, and a reading that AI is potentially too capital-intensive.

Of course, the consensus may be too conservative in the top line for the next three years by linearly implying something that may become non-linear. In any case, the temporal mismatch remains the central point: CapEx upfront and gradual revenue, creating the illusion of weak return.

The Second Engine Is Starting to Work

Seeking to measure progress in terms of monetization of Microsoft's application layer, here it can already be said firsthand that the signals are stronger than the market seems to price.

In FQ2, Microsoft reported 15 million Microsoft 365 Copilot Seats, which is basically more than 160% YoY growth—already very real monetization. In terms of ARPU, the numbers are going up, and Copilot is arguably a relevant driver. Although Microsoft does not explicitly report ARPU in dollars, if we consider M365 commercial cloud revenue growing 17% YoY and paid Seats growing 6% YoY in FQ2, then I believe ARPU growth would be in the range of ~11%. On the consumer side, M365 Consumer Cloud is growing revenues at 29% YoY and 6% in subscribers, which would also imply an ARPU growth of ~23%.

The general reading is that, arguably, ARPU has grown well above MSFT's track record, and not only from price increases but also from structural E5 upsell and Copilot monetization. The main point of skepticism is that despite the percentage growth of ARPU, the still low penetration of 15 million versus a base of 450 million makes it clear whether an early saturation of this trend could occur.

The Multiple Reset Isn’t Random

According to YCharts, Microsoft today trades at trailing and forward earnings multiples very close to recent lows of ~22x, while expected long-term growth remains relatively stable between ~13-14%. A basic reading would be that the multiple has fallen, but the growth has not fallen as much, so MSFT is cheap.

But embedded in this correction is possibly something beyond a mere re-rating for profit taking (for example), such as a compression multiple for uncertainty in timing (in the case of CapEx flowing into Azure) and quality (in the sustainability of ARPU driven by the application layers).

In a way, the previous chart explains that the market continues to believe in ~14% growth, but is not sure exactly how it will come. Therefore, in the short to medium term, I don't believe in a reversal of bearish momentum and compression multiple as long as the market doesn't see more clearly the ROI appearing in Azure relative to CapEx, or else if AI contribution will finally be better quantified.

If FQ3 brings an Azure growth going from ~39% YoY to the low/mid 40s or guidance implying acceleration, this would already calm the spirits in terms of ROI. Perhaps more importantly, if there are CapEx signals signaling a peak, or even more controlled guidance, this alone can re-rate MSFT back to mid-2025 levels. In terms of the application layer, ARPU accelerating or sustaining double-digit growth with revenue growing well above seats proves real pricing power, and I believe it helps paint a better picture for MSFT.

The market has been operating with slightly positive expectations in the last three months for FQ3, not necessarily due to a lack of growth but rather visibility—the point I would most like to see addressed in Microsoft's FQ3 earnings call.

In Limbo Until the Numbers Catch Up

There is obscurity in the actual disclosure of the main points of skepticism in the market today about Microsoft's thesis: (1) AI contribution within Azure; (2) conversion of CapEx into future revenue; (3) AI unit economics; and (4) sustainability of ARPU in Copilot.

Even if the signs in the application layer are already beginning to appear in a more concrete way, the infrastructure layer still carries a greater weight of a cycle of massive investment whose return remains more implicit than visible. And the result of this is a natural misalignment between narrative and numbers, where part of the growth may already be priced in at a $2.74 trillion market cap, although not yet fully proven.

That being said, as long as there is no greater clarity on the part of the management team about the points to be questioned by the market, I see Microsoft in a kind of "limbo", unless I am mistaken if FQ3—to be reported in the coming weeks—brings a relevant improvement in visibility, either via (1) clearer acceleration of Azure, (2) better quantification of AI contribution, or (3) signs of greater discipline in CapEx.

So, how can we be sure that it will have a better entry point for Microsoft's thesis? I would approach it by looking at moving averages.

The chart below shows that at $369 per share, MSFT trades below the 20-day and 50-day moving averages, and well below the 200-day moving averages, i.e., it is in a downtrend on all timeframes. In this case, since I don't have a crystal ball to guess the bottom, I believe the key here is to confirm a regime change to rebuild a position.

Let's say that FQ3 is well received by the market, and there is a reclaim of the 20-day MA. This will show that the selling pressure has lost strength, which is the first sign. The second signal is the reclaim above the 50-day MA, which in this case confirms that buying flow is returning and that a reversal may be starting, this being the "early signal" I am looking for. Counterintuitively, going long on MSFT when it is coming back to or above 200MA tends to have a better risk-return, even if you pay more, but it eliminates much of the risk of continued decline.

The bottom line is not about paying the lowest price but paying for the moment when the thesis works again. I remain cautious about buying MSFT at the current pullback, and the Hold rating is my stance.

data.title: Good Jobs Numbers Offset By Likely Lengthy Wartime Risk
data.description:
NEW YORK (April 3) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) today reported 186,000 new private sector jobs were created in March, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The government reduced the number of jobs it provided by 8,000, so that total nonfarm jobs printed at 178,000 net new jobs.

January and February revisions resulted in a net 7,000 jobs lost.

For the three months from December 2025, average monthly jobs creation has been just 68,000 jobs per month, affirming our view in prior jobs and GDP reports that the economy is slowing.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed worse than the Establishment Survey, showing 64,000 jobs lost

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate were 4.3% and 8.0%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part-time for economic reasons, plus all people marginally attached to the labor force. Some 386,000 people left the civilian labor force. The Labor Force Participation Rate fell nonetheless, to just 61.9%, the lowest since November 2021.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of February (in red) and March (in green) Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

In keeping with our habit for jobs reports of a month concluding a quarterly period, we present here the average real wages by occupation:

The War Effect and the Economy

The onset of the Iran War has significantly altered the outlook for the economy. Any war where the US is a combatant would, but a war in the Persian Gulf is to the global economy what open-heart surgery is to a human being. Just as the heart is the source of human blood flow, so, too, is the Persian Gulf - and particularly the Strait of Hormuz - the source of the global economy’s flow of oil. Gasoline and diesel fuel prices have increased significantly even from the already elevated prices we reported in our February jobs report, according to this chart adopted from the AAA:

Time Frame

Regular

Mid-Grade

Premium

Diesel

Today

$4.09

$4.60

$4.97

$5.53

From our February Jobs Report

$3.32

$3.82

$4.18

$4.33

Crude oil futures have also spiked to their highest level in a decade, as seen in the SA Chart linked here. In addition, some of the Gulf countries have cited the war as force majeure to escape lower pricing in their supply contracts so that they can jack up pricing on their customers.

Those higher fuel prices will be felt throughout the economy.

Though there is some debate, it is generally observed that spikes in fuel prices tend to be disinflationary, and even deflationary, at least in the government statistics roundup, because the Fed’s reported preferred measure of inflation, Core PCE inflation, excludes food and fuel. Since consumers’ disposable income will likely be eaten up by higher fuel prices, less is left for them to spend on consumer staples and discretionary items. (We already see some of this in the disposable personal income figures where spending has exceeded disposable income, in the "Other Data Points" section of this report.) Thus, while “headline” inflation figures, like the Consumer Price Index, will print higher, core inflation will likely be reduced.

The market has largely gone “risk-off” because of the war. The global economy has been negatively affected as well, as Iran continues to threaten transshipments of oil from the Persian Gulf by bottling up the Strait of Hormuz. Reports indicate that Kuwait has ceased production because its oil storage facilities are full. Iraq has cut production as well, and Qatar is threatening the same.

One-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas supply has been cut off, and the cash flow to the aforementioned countries has been cut off. While the tactical performance of the US forces in the Gulf theater has been utterly spectacular, it remains unclear what the mission of the war is or how its political objectives can be achieved by military means. The president’s messaging on the war has been erratic, even mercurial, with ill-defined strategic objectives ranging from eliminating Iran’s nuclear capabilities, to eliminating Iran’s missile production, to regime change, to unconditional surrender to, now, removing 400kg or enriched uranium. Of late, the president seems to be hoping to leave the region with the strait still subject to Iranian control and asking other nations that are more reliant on Persian Gulf oil to free the strait from Iranian control.

For the markets, unless and until the political objectives of the mission and the military means to achieve them become better defined so that markets can assess whether we are approaching a cessation of hostilities, we predict the VIX chart will resemble the Comet rollercoaster at Coney Island.

Speaking for ourselves, we don’t see the war concluding favorably or soon, largely because it violates so much of Clausewitz’s "Wondrous Trinity," often summarized as "People, Army, Government". His theory requires all three to act in tandem, and we see at least two - people and government - failing right now.

  • First, polls show the political support for the war is weak and waning, so the passion of the trinity is weak, at best.
  • Second, while the US has been successful in its strikes in Iran, our service members and weaponry in the region remain at risk, as do we in the homeland from the chance attack by Iranian “sleeper” cells of terrorists.
  • Finally, as we’ve stated, the government rationale for the war has been mutable.

We don’t see the situation being resolved in weeks or even months until all three elements of the trinity act in concert. Consequently, we see the war likely lasting into 2027 at least. Even diplomatic efforts will remain challenging after Iran rejected a proposal to meet in Islamabad.

Moreover, as analysts at Thompson-Reuters note, Iran's navy is not the one we sunk but the navy Iran built to deny navigation of the Strait of Hormuz by a hostile power: the Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy, or IRGCN. It is comprised of coastal missile batteries; hundreds of widely dispersed fast attack boats docked in caves along the Hormuz shoreline, many of which carry torpedoes; small, cheap, attack drones; and over 5,000 sea mines. (The U.S. Navy's minesweeping capabilities are severely limited at the moment).

Needless to say, lengthy hostile foreign entanglements are anathema to the American public, which expresses its frustration in the polls.

The Economy

The war highlights that the Fed has botched rates for several meetings and that private, business-oriented hiring - outside the healthcare and education sector - has slowed considerably.

We note, too, that the job opening rate, per the February Beveridge Curve, is 4.2%, while the unemployment rate is 4.4%. That continues a trend from August of unemployment exceeding jobs openings. Prior to that, from May 2021, the job openings percentage exceeded the unemployment rate. It should be noted that the labor force participation rate is 61.9%, the lowest since November 2021. That’s counterintuitive, because we expected the rate to increase in 2026 due to work requirements for SNAP recipients. (Work requirements for Medicaid start in 2027.)

The “green shoots” we had hoped to see coming in the latter quarters of this year from capex from reshoring have likely dissipated because of the Supreme Court’s tariff decision. We may still see significant gains in the construction sector coming from AI data center construction, but local opposition to such builds will delay job growth from them into later quarters or years. That said, we note that the ISM Manufacturing Index, discussed and linked below, is in its third month of “growth” after a near year of "contraction.

Average job creation over the last three months has been just 68,000 jobs per month.

Core CPE inflation has stepped up somewhat in January, to 3.1%. Part of that has been rough winter weather in population centers in the Northeast and northern Central States; some has occurred because of major healthcare worker strikes.

The Fed's discount rate should probably be about 2.25 to 2.75%, about 100 bps lower than present. Even if it was that low, the discount rate has little effect on longer-term rates, which are the real problem for job creation and home ownership.

We reiterate our desire to see the incoming Fed chair, Kevin Warsh, engage in yield curve control, at least until hostilities with Iran cease, so that longer-term rates can be reduced. Alternatively (or, perhaps, in conjunction with that monetary policy), we would like to see Congress enact fiscal policy that would exempt Treasurys entirely or partially from Federal Income Tax.

Yield curve control sets longer-term rates within thresholds set by the Fed, with the Fed buying and selling to keep such rates within the targeted range. Exempting Treasury interest from Federal Income Tax would increase demand, raise bond prices, and thereby lower rates. In addition, since rates would decrease, it would help ameliorate some of the deficit since the interest on the federal debt could be reduced. (We did this during World War I to control rates and inflation, so it’s not unprecedented.) It would, however, obviously “horn in” on the municipal bond market, so that rates there would increase and cause higher yields since they would not be as attractive as the federal bonds.)

Prognostications

We reiterate our view of the last several months that the data point to a slowing economy. PCE spending is slowing; however, we think there may be substantially increased GDI owing to the Big Beautiful Bill, but that won’t be reflected, likely, until at least 2026Q3. That, plus the consequences of the Iran War, will make the midterm elections favor the Democrats. Given the mood of the Democrat caucus, it’s almost certain the president would, again, be impeached and his MAGA agenda scuttled.

While today’s jobs release, we are nevertheless further downgrading our estimate of 2026 Q1 to 0.75%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the same for 2026Q2 for now, but it will depend on the length of the war. As stated above, we are not optimistic about an early resolution of the conflict. We expect the Fed will stand pat with interest rates until the new chair, Kevin Warsh, takes the helm for the June meeting. But war developments could cause the Fed to have an emergency meeting to radically reduce rates, as it did after 9/11.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at just 0.7% in 2025Q4, according to the Second Estimate released March 13th. Core GDP, or real final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at a moribund 0.5%.

The personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index for January, released March 13th, increased 2.8% from January 2025. Excluding food and energy prices, the PCE price index increased 3.1%. Core PCE inflation is, reportedly, the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) for March, released Wednesday, printed up slightly, at 52.7, up from 52.4. This is the third month of a growth trend from a prior 11 month contraction. The February Services Index, released this morning, printed at 56.1, a 230 bps increase from January, with most sectors showing growth.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for February, released Tuesday, printed 358,000 fewer job openings than in January.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in January were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,376,000. This is 5.4 percent below the revised December rate of 1,455,000 and is 5.8 percent below the January 2025 rate of 1,460,000. Privately-owned housing starts in January were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,487,000. This is 7.2 percent (±13.7 percent)* above the revised December estimate of 1,387,000 and is 9.5 percent (±16.5 percent)* above the January 2025 rate of 1,358,000.

Personal income increased $113.8 billion (0.4 percent at a monthly rate) in January, according to estimates released March 13th by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $219.9 billion (0.9 percent), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $81.1 billion (0.4 percent).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for March printed at 47.5, continuing a down trend..

______________________________________________

AUTHOR'S NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe are underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies that we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

data.title: Market Brief: Silver's Physical Tightness Is A Bullish Signal
data.description:
COMEX (US) silver's registered inventory has fallen to 13-14% coverage of outstanding open interest, while March 2026 delivery was unusually high and SHFE (Shanghai) futures now trade at a 12% premium to COMEX, together signaling extreme tightness that creates upside pressure on silver price.

Silver reached a record near $121.64/oz in January 2026, then pulled back sharply into the high $60s and low $70s. However, the price selloff and the physical delivery data moved in divergent directions.

Physical silver continued leaving COMEX vaults, which is the world's primary price-setting venue for silver and the benchmark against which global physical supply is measured, which makes its registered inventory the most direct observable proxy for deliverable supply stress.

The Registered Inventory Tightness

COMEX silver inventory is divided into two categories. Registered silver carries a warehouse warrant and is immediately available for delivery against a futures contract. Eligible silver is stored in COMEX-approved vaults and meets exchange quality standards but is not currently warranted for delivery; it requires re-registration before it can fulfill a contract.

Eligible metal can become registered if the owner chooses to issue a warehouse warrant, making it available for delivery. However, eligible silver is privately owned and the owner must voluntarily decide to issue a warrant and convert it. While the paperwork is fast (usually 24–48 hours), the decision is entirely up to the owner. They may be holding it for their own clients or long-term investment, have it pledged as collateral elsewhere, or simply not want to release it into the delivery pool during a squeeze.

Only registered silver provides the exchange's actual delivery buffer. As of late March 2026, registered stocks stood at approximately 76.0M oz. Against that figure, total silver futures open interest was approximately 576M oz, implying a coverage ratio of 13.4%.

A coverage ratio below 15% is the threshold that exchange analysts historically flag as stress territory. The current reading sits just below that level, not at catastrophic lows, but with limited margin for incremental demand.

March Delivery Was Unusually Large

The March 2026 delivery cycle drew approximately 9,212 contracts, equal to roughly 46.1M oz of physical silver. To contextualize that figure: 46.1M oz against 76.0M oz of current registered inventory represents approximately 60.6% of current available registered stocks absorbed in a single delivery month.

The registered inventory chart at the top of this article shows the cumulative effect: a sustained drawdown in warranted stocks that began accelerating in late 2025 and has continued through the March delivery cycle.

The SHFE Premium Confirms the Physical Bid Is Not Local

A persistent premium in Shanghai futures (SHFE) over COMEX is another signal for tightness in the silver market. As of April 1, 2026, SHFE silver futures traded at approximately $84.59/oz. COMEX May/Jun futures on the same date were quoted at $74.94/oz. The spread: roughly $9–10/oz, or 12–13%.

A spread of this magnitude, sustained since late 2025, is very significant. Buyers in Asia are pricing silver above the COMEX level, and they are not arbitraging away the premium, demonstrating the supply is genuinely constrained. The sustained premium in Shanghai also coincides with the acceleration in COMEX delivery demand.

Price Outlook: Bullish Direction, Unstable Path

The structural case for higher silver prices rests on three converging signals: coverage ratio, delivery velocity, and cross-market premium. As registered stocks decline relative to open interest, the market has less capacity to absorb incremental physical demand at current prices. Elevated March delivery demand, if sustained, continues to compress that buffer. And the SHFE premium confirms the physical bid is not a COMEX-specific artifact, it is global.

When a 12–13% premium persists between the world's two largest silver futures venues, one of two things eventually happens: COMEX prices rise to close the gap, or supply flows from West to East until the premium compresses. Both outcomes are bullish for COMEX silver. The first is direct repricing. The second drains additional metal from an already thin registered stock pool.

However, it should also be noted that the near-term path is not clean. Paper liquidation, particularly in a macro risk-off environment, can continue to push futures lower regardless of what physical inventories are doing.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: INNOVATE Q4: MediBeacon Sale Could Be Only Saving Grace, Downgrade To Sell
data.description:
I covered INNOVATE Corp. (VATE) more than a year ago, when it was trading just under $13. About a month before that, the FDA had approved its TGFR system, and by the time of my coverage, the stock had run up 150 percent. My rating was a Hold at that time, my reason being we were still some quarters away from the monitors being... well, monetized.

In the year following my article, the stock dropped below $4, chiefly, I believe, due to the low visibility provided by management over that period.

The FDA approval was announced in the Q4 2024 release on March 31, 2025, along with news of China's NMPA approving the monitor and the sensor for the CKD (Chronic Kidney Disease) indication and AKI (Acute Kidney Injury).

In Q1 2025, management simply reported that it had "started the year on the front foot" and that "MediBeacon looks forward to exploring potential applications with clinicians both in the hospital and outpatient settings".

Two quarters later, in Q3 2025, management was still talking about "we continue to see positive momentum" and "MediBeacon received regulatory approval to sell the Transdermal GFR System in China".

If that were the entire story, I may not have written this article, but about a week ago, readers started commenting about the stock being on fire, and it piqued my curiosity that the stock would jump to $7.77, where it stands as I write this.

Why now? Were there significant catalysts in the making?

The answer was in the Q4 2025 earnings call from a little over a week ago, and two things stood out for me.

One was the announcement of MediBeacon's U.S. commercial roll-out to at least one COE, which is what investors have been waiting for. MediBeacon is their FDA-approved transdermal glomerular filtration rate testing device and system.

The second catalyst is the DBM backlog now being reported at $1.8 billion. An associated data point was the 12.5 percent revenue increase for the quarter, on a year-on-year basis. DBM Global is the key component of its Infrastructure segment.

In addition to this, the device now has the CE mark for the EU, and Lumitrace, the tracer agent for the device, is approved both in the U.S. and in China, with the EU application still pending.

I have written detailed descriptions of each of their segments in my previous article, so please feel free to read this and come back to our narrative here.

It would appear that the market is now more bullish because the promised momentum from the TGFR system entering the clinic is finally here. I shall now explore the quarter's key developments and try to plot a trajectory for the stock moving forward, but my spoiler is that I have downgraded my rating on VATE from Hold to Sell. Let us find out why.

Q4 Results and Forward Implications

Revenue came in at $382.7 million for Q4 and $1.246 billion for FY 2025, which we saw was a year-on-year growth rate of 12.5 percent. About $1.21 billion of that was from the infrastructure segment, and we shall discuss that separately, but the life sciences segment that MediBeacon belongs to saw a 27.6 percent uplift to $12.5 million. Obviously, that is not a significant figure by any measure, but it does exhibit signs of momentum building up as commercialization begins to gain traction.

Infrastructure results were impressive, no doubt, but I think the fuel for the stock price surge came from the $700 million increase in adjusted backlog, which is a term used for "awarded but not yet signed contracts". Of the total $1.784 billion, $1.724 billion is on a reported basis, meaning signed contracts.

While that is certainly good news, I think what is even more meaningful is the 'life' being shown in life sciences. This is where the real future upside lies, in my opinion, and that has not changed since my last article from more than a year ago:

most of the potential upside will very likely come from the monitoring systems unit with the brand new FDA feather in its cap

The revenue bump for Life Sciences does not tell us much because the product is still in the COE stage, and the bump has likely come from the Chinese partner for R2 Technologies' Glacier products. However, we do know that the company has:

secured our first TGFR System order from a top-tier academic medical center and as inventory continues to build we expect to drive additional placements across similar leading institutions. These centers will play a critical role in demonstrating the value of our technology in real-world settings, particularly in high-impact use cases such as kidney transplantation as well as optimizing drug dosing in oncology and cardiology through more precise real-time assessment of kidney function.

So how big is MediBeacon expected to be?

If one goes back several years to the Q4 2020 earnings call, one sees that the total addressable market is significantly higher than what management pegged it at, at the time:

The potential market is in excess of $7 billion and the technology employed by MediBeacon can be used for other areas of medicine besides the kidney.

Despite the commercialization timeline still being in its early stages, the COE sales launch in the U.S. is a crucial one because it brings validation for the technology and the product. This is where VATE can build its KOL (key opinion leader) base ahead of large-scale commercialization.

If MediBeacon manages to capture even a meager 5 percent of that $7 billion market, that is $350 million. Put into the right context, that is more than 3x the current market capitalization of $106 million.

However, there is an even more daunting mountain to scale in the form of indebtedness, and for this reason, a sale of the MediBeacon subsidiary has become a very real outcome. This makes the COE stage a critical one because it has the potential to drive this asset's value higher. The FDA approval in the U.S., the CE Mark approval in the EU, the China approvals - all these developments have the effect of adding premiums to MediBeacon's price tag.

It has now come to a point where the sale might be VATE's only saving grace as it attempts to scale its wall of debt.

The Debt Cloud Hangs Heavy

Why? I think the main reason is that commercialization is an expensive ordeal, especially in multiple markets. That points directly to the financial strength of the company, and that is not in great shape.

Although revenues for FY 2025 were up 12.5 percent, helped by Q4 2025's growth rate of nearly 62 percent, VATE still posted net losses of $64 million for the full fiscal and $7.8 million for the fourth quarter.

Admittedly, these losses have been improving quarter-over-quarter through the fiscal year, and this has significantly strengthened operating cash flows over that period. However, if you read into the cash flow statements, these OCF increases have primarily come from positive working capital adjustments on the balance sheet over the past two of those quarters. The AR/AP prints were both positive, which means VATE is realizing invoices at a faster rate while holding AP cash for longer.

One cannot view this as long-term financial stability, particularly when one looks at VATE's looming Holdco debt maturities and associated covenants that have largely gone past their deadlines.

In November 2025, three months after the refinancing of its 8.5 percent notes in August 2025, VATE initiated a sales process for its DBM business. On March 9, 2026, S&P downgraded VATE to CCC- and then promptly pulled back the rating "at the issuer's request".

VATE has already defaulted on the Feb 1 covenant deadline, and as of the March 26 10-K and 8-K filings, neither DBM nor Spectrum asset sales have been announced. And yet, Interim CEO Paul Voight said on the earnings call that:

We have either met, received waivers or extended the stated milestones across our businesses.

That only pushes the can down the road and tightens any "extended" covenants it may entail.

Against a CC&E of $112.1 million as of Q4 2025:

INNOVATE had principal outstanding indebtedness of $687.2 million, up $18.9 million from $668.3 million at the end of 2024, driven by the indebtedness refinancing transactions at our non-operating and Life Sciences segments, which was partially offset by a decrease in Infrastructure's outstanding debt.

The problem here is that much of this $112 million is from the DBM unit because it is still the only (barely) profitable segment, which means the parent holding company cannot immediately access this to service parent debt. The 10-K also included this going concern statement:

As of the date of these financial statements, there is substantial doubt about the Company's ability to continue as a going concern within one year after the date that the financial statements are issued. The principal conditions leading to this conclusion are the upcoming maturities of our debt obligations. Based on these conditions, the Company may not be able to meet its obligations at maturity and comply with certain cross-default provisions under the 2027 Senior Secured Notes over the next twelve months, or any potential breach of the milestone covenant of our 2027 Senior Secured Notes, which has required the Company to commence a sales process for all or substantially all of DBM Global’s assets or equity interests in accordance with certain dates and deadlines.

Summarizing the Thesis

After a significant amount of deliberation on my part, I downgrade VATE to Sell. A DBM sale could potentially help pay down debt and bring the parent's books up to a healthy level, but if that should happen, there would still be the issue of multi-market commercialization for MediBeacon's products that would force the parent to take on fresh debt. I do not see this happening, nor do I foresee a sale of MediBeacon being good for the holding company because this is where significant future revenues lie. Without it, and in the absence of looming debt maturities, VATE could potentially survive as an infrastructure and spectrum company, with some traction from R2. Even so, it would not be immediately profitable, and a long road to recovery would lie ahead of it.

The biggest reason for the downgrade, therefore, is a potential sale of MediBeacon assets. With little to no visibility past this point and management not helping with its "either met, received waivers, or extended the stated milestones" statement, I foresee a high probability that it will lose its MediBeacon business to a takeover. The DBM unit is certainly an attractive one for any buyer, but MediBeacon is where the bacon is. The more distressed the company's financials get over time, the more leverage buyers will have at the negotiation table.

This is not a narrative one wants to be a part of. If your cost basis for VATE shares is under the current price, I would advise selling the stock and walking away relatively unscathed. Others may want to double down on the company and continue holding their shares, and those who added to their positions or initiated one after Q4 2025 earnings were out are not likely to back out this soon. However, considering that the forward look is heavily skewed toward risk rather than reward, it may not be wise to stay in the game when one is at the very bottom of the capital stack.
